result:
There is a specific kind of magic in the transition between seasons, particularly the slow exhale from summer into autumn. It doesn't happen all at once; it’s a gradual surrender. One morning, the air just feels crisper, carrying a faint scent of woodsmoke and dried leaves that wasn't there the day before. The light changes too, shifting from the harsh, white glare of July to a softer, golden hue that stretches out across the late afternoon like spilled honey. 

I love this in-between space. Summer is loud and demanding, full of obligations to be outside and soak up the sun before it vanishes. But autumn? Autumn is permissive. It grants you the grace to stay indoors without guilt. It is the season of the second chance, the time when the world lets go of what it no longer needs—just look at the trees shedding their leaves without a second thought. There’s a quiet bravery in that bareness.

We spend so much of our lives clinging to things: people, places, versions of ourselves that no longer fit. Watching the seasons turn is a gentle reminder that endings are just the earth making room for something new. As the chill settles into the bones of the old house and the kettle whistles on the stove, there is a profound comfort in knowing that sometimes, the most beautiful thing you can do is simply let go.
